data.title: Goolsbee sees danger of rising inflation expectations after two price shocks - report
data.description:
After a one-two punch of tariffs and surging oil prices sparked the Iran war, the Federal Reserve may face increasing skepticism from the American public over its ability to bring inflation back to its 2% target, according to the head of the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago.

"If we’re going to have high oil prices, we may start seeing consumer inflation expectations rising notably. And to have piled this thing on before the tariff inflation went away, that’s a double danger," Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said in an interview with the Financial Times.

Inflation has remained above the Fed's target for more than four years. "Anytime you’re floating around well above the [inflation] target for an extended period of time, there’s a danger," he said. The two successive price shocks may be "misinterpreted by consumers or businesses as something persistent."

That becomes especially worrisome as consumers' expectations of future inflation are often linked to gasoline prices, according to research from economist Yuriy Gorodnichenko that Goolsbee cited.

The central bank's policymakers generally look through one-time price increases on the basis that they don't create persistent upward pressure on inflation.

While he doesn't vote on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, he said he wants to see evidence that inflation is resuming its path toward the 2% target before he supports interest rate cuts.

data.title: BRP tariff warning weighs on recreational vehicle space
data.description:
In the wake of BRP’s (DOO) decision to pull FY27 guidance due to changes in tariff costs on powersport products, shares within the sector are trading defensively.

The maker of Sea-Doo jet skis, Lynx snowmobiles, and Can-Am all-terrain vehicles warned that the recent amendment of Section 232 tariffs on steel, aluminum, and copper imports into the U.S. will result in an incremental tariff cost of $500M for the remainder of 2026 before any mitigation efforts. This led the company to suspend guidance for 2027.

"Like many manufacturers, we are operating in a highly volatile and unpredictable tariff environment that continues to create uncertainty across the market," said Denis Le Vot, President and CEO of BRP.

The change in Section 232 now means BRP (DOO) will now pay a 25% tariff on the total value of the imported vehicle rather than a 50% tariff on applicable metal content only. 

BRP (DOO) manufactures a majority of its products in Mexico, Canada, and Finland, with Rotax engines manufactured in Austria through a joint venture between BRP and Rotax GmbH & Co KG.  Only the aluminum casings for its vehicles are made in the U.S.

This development is weighing on peers, with shares of Polaris (PII) suffering its largest one-day percentage decline in a year, with notable losses for Patrick Industries (PATK), Malibu Boats (MBUU), and Twin Vee Power Cats (VEEE).

data.title: S&P 500 surpasses 7,000 as investors stay optimistic about Middle East developments
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages advanced on Wednesday as investors awaited good news on the U.S.-Iran conflict front while parsing more bank and financial earnings.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is tightly close to its all-time high of 7,002.28 and was last +0.5%.

The heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last +1.2%, aided by Broadcom (AVGO), +3.5%, as it entered into a three-year partnership with Meta Platforms ((META)) to develop its AI chips and deploy them into data centers.

Nonetheless, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) was -0.4%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 4 basis points higher at 4.29%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.79%.

“The 2-year Treasury yield ((US2Y)) fell below 3.75% today (the upper bound of the fed funds rate) for the first time since March 17th as the likelihood of a rate cut by year-end has increased,” said Collin Martin, head of fixed income research and strategy at Schwab Research. “The outlook is highly uncertain, and there's a lot of time between now and the December meeting. For now, we expect the Fed to remain on hold for the next several meetings.”

On geopolitics, President Donald Trump hinted that negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan. This was after the U.S. announced a move towards a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Reports surfaced that the U.S. and Iran may have made significant progress towards a framework agreement to end the war, according to U.S. officials.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $91, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $95 per barrel.

On the economy, U.S. export prices climbed faster than U.S. import prices in March, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Also, the Empire State Manufacturing Index rose 11 points to 11.0 in April, vs. the -2.0 consensus, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York reported.

data.title: American Strategic Investment outlines potential sales of 123 William Street and 196 Orchard while maintaining 80.3% occupancy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Strategic Investment Co. (NYC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • "We remain committed to operating and unlocking value at our current assets with a focus on tenant retention, property improvements and cost efficiency, while simultaneously pruning our exposure to non-core assets" (Chief Executive Officer Nicholas Schorsch).
  • "For the year, we executed 13 new and replacement leases totaling 117,000 square feet" (CEO Schorsch). He also described the portfolio at year-end as "our $382.6 million, 0.7 million square foot portfolio" with "occupancy of 80.3% and a weighted average remaining lease term of 6.1 years" (CEO Schorsch).
  • "As discussed on last quarter's call, we completed the disposition of our 1140 Avenue of the Americas office property during the fourth quarter" (CEO Schorsch). He added, "we also pursued a cooperative consensual foreclosure with the lender" and said the company "recognized a gain of $46.6 million" for the year (CEO Schorsch).
  • "Revenue was $43.3 million for the year ended December 31, 2025, compared to $61.6 million in 2024" (CFO Michael LeSanto). He attributed the change to dispositions, "notably the dispositions of 9 Times Square in the late fourth quarter of 2024, and the 1140 Avenue of the Americas in fourth quarter 2025" (CFO LeSanto).

Outlook

  • "We are also assessing strategies for our properties at 123 William Street and 196 Orchard to generate the greatest long-term value for our portfolio, including potentially selling the properties" (CEO Schorsch). He said, "If sold, these sales would generate additional cash that we believe can be deployed into higher-yielding assets, creating future value for the portfolio" (CEO Schorsch).
  • "Simultaneously, our team is focused on leasing up available space, evaluating options for replacing maturing debt, renewing leases with existing tenants and maintaining tight controls on expenses" (CEO Schorsch).
  • The call did not include management quantitative guidance (no explicit revenue, earnings, or EBITDA targets were provided for 2026).

Financial Results

  • "Revenue for the fourth quarter 2025 was $6.5 million compared to $14.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2024" (CFO LeSanto).
  • "The company's full year GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders was $21.2 million compared to a net loss of $140.6 million in 2024" (CFO LeSanto). "Net loss for the quarter was $6.7 million, in line with the $6.7 million we recorded in the fourth quarter of 2024" (CFO LeSanto).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA for 2025 was $0.3 million and $1.2 million for the fourth quarter" and "Cash NOI for the full year was $16 million and $1.8 million in the fourth quarter" (CFO LeSanto).
  • Balance sheet and debt framing emphasized rate protection and near-term maturities: "The company's balance sheet includes 100% fixed rate debt and prudent net leverage of 47.5%" and "We ended the fourth quarter with net debt of $249.7 million at a weighted average effective interest rate of 4.5% and a weighted average remaining debt term of 1.5 years" (CFO LeSanto).

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: No analyst questions were included; analyst sentiment could not be assessed from the prepared-remarks-only format.
  • Management: Prepared remarks leaned slightly positive on portfolio stability and repositioning; "We believe that this term, coupled with a high-quality, largely investment-grade tenant base provides significant portfolio stability" (CEO Schorsch), alongside execution-focused language on dispositions and expense controls.
  • Versus last quarter: Management continued emphasizing balance-sheet actions and asset sales; the current quarter added completion language ("we completed the disposition") rather than expected/anticipated framing.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Portfolio and leasing: Q4 cited "occupancy of 80.3%" and "a weighted average remaining lease term of 6.1 years" (CEO Schorsch) versus Q3 stating the lease term was "6.2 years" at quarter end (CEO Schorsch, Q3). Q4 said "57% of our leases now extend beyond 2030, up from 56% last quarter" (CEO Schorsch) compared to Q3’s "56% of our leases now extend beyond 2030" (CEO Schorsch, Q3).
  • Dispositions and balance sheet actions: Q3 described 1140 Avenue of the Americas as "anticipated to close in the fourth quarter of 2025" and "expected to eliminate a $99 million liability that matures in July 2026" (CEO Schorsch, Q3). Q4 shifted to completion and accounting impact, saying the company "removed the related assets and liabilities from our balance sheet" and "recognized a gain of $46.6 million" (CEO Schorsch).
  • Communication focus: Q3 included a cost-structure action on professional fees and auditor change (CFO LeSanto, Q3), while Q4 centered on debt profile (fixed-rate, leverage, net debt, and remaining debt term) and potential additional property sales (CFO LeSanto; CEO Schorsch).

Risks and Concerns

  • Disposition execution and reinvestment dependence were explicit in management’s conditional language: "If sold, these sales would generate additional cash" and proceeds "can be deployed into higher-yielding assets" (CEO Schorsch).
  • Refinancing and maturity management remained a near-term operational focus: "evaluating options for replacing maturing debt" (CEO Schorsch) alongside disclosure that the weighted average remaining debt term was "1.5 years" (CFO LeSanto).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q4 2025 around portfolio stability metrics, completion of the 1140 Avenue of the Americas disposition via consensual foreclosure (including a $46.6 million gain), and a continued pivot toward targeted dispositions and redeployment, with a specific focus on evaluating potential sales of 123 William Street and 196 Orchard while maintaining tight expense control and preparing to address upcoming debt maturities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Minerva launched with New Outperform at Citizens on schizophrenia candidate
data.description:
Minerva Neurosciences (NERV) gained on Wednesday after Citizens launched its coverage with a Market Outperform recommendation, citing a blockbuster opportunity for the company’s lead candidate, roluperidone, which is undergoing studies for negative symptoms of schizophrenia.

Analyst Jason Butler pointed out that Minerva (NERV) is advancing roluperidone in a Phase 3 confirmatory trial, which is expected to generate data in H2 2027. Subject to positive results, the once-daily therapy could be the first and only U.S.-approved treatment for the condition, he argued, with a $14 target on NERV.

“Roluperidone has the potential to be the first and only approved therapy specifically targeting negative symptoms of schizophrenia, representing a blockbuster opportunity, in our view,” Butler wrote.

The initial target market for roluperidone could be about 650K schizophrenia patients with pronounced negative symptoms but stable positive symptoms, the analyst added, projecting about $1.9B in peak U.S. sales for the drug by 2034. The estimate is based on a 65% probability for approval and a 20% peak penetration among the targeted population.

data.title: Qatar warns Iran war will have major global economic impact
data.description:
Qatari Finance Minister Ali bin Ahmed Al Kuwari has warned the world will feel the full impact of the Iran war in one to two months.

“Full-fledged impact is coming in one or two months,” Al Kuwari said during an appearance at an International Monetary Fund meeting in Washington, according to Bloomberg. “You’ll see huge economic impact as a result of this war.”

Al Kuwari said that escalating fuel prices due to the war were "the tip of the iceberg," noting that exports of helium, which is used in chip production, have also been disrupted. Qatar supplies around 30% of the world's helium.

Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility, which accounts for roughly 20% of global LNG exports, was badly damaged last month by an Iranian missile strike. 

In his remarks, Al Kuwari also warned of possible food shortages due to the disruption of fertilizer shipments from the Persian Gulf.

Al Kuwari estimated it could take up to five years for Qatar to fully restore exports.

data.title: U.S., Iran weigh two-week extension of ceasefire - report
data.description:
The U.S. and Iran are considering whether to extend their current ceasefire by two weeks to allow more time for peace negotiations, according to a media report on Wednesday.

Mediators between the two sides are working to arrange technical talks addressing the most contentious issues, including reopening the Strait of Hormuz and Iran’s nuclear enrichment program, Bloomberg reported, citing a person familiar. The current fragile ceasefire is set to expire on Tuesday.

If successful, these technical discussions could lay the groundwork for the next round of negotiations between senior officials from both countries.

However, an extension is not guaranteed. A U.S. official said Washington has not yet agreed to the proposal, Bloomberg reported, noting Iran’s foreign ministry did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Another person familiar with the talks noted that neither the U.S. nor Iran wants to return to fighting.

On Tuesday, U.S. President Donald Trump suggested that renewed negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan.

data.title: M&T outlines $7.2B-$7.35B 2026 NII outlook while moving CET1 target range to 10%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: M&T Bank (MTB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Net interest margin expanded 2 basis points,” and “C&I growth was strong with average C&I loans growing at $1.5 billion from the fourth quarter,” Daryl Bible said (Senior EVP & CFO Daryl Bible), while also citing “fee income remains a bright spot, growing 13% from the first quarter of 2025” and “more than $700 million reduction in criticized balances.”
  • Bible said M&T “executed $1.25 billion in share repurchases, representing over 3.5% of shares outstanding as of the end of 2025,” and reported “diluted GAAP earnings per share were $4.13, down from $4.67 in the prior quarter.”
  • “Taxable equivalent net interest income was $1.76 billion,” and “net interest margin was 3.71%,” Bible said (Senior EVP & CFO Bible), attributing margin drivers to fixed-rate repricing and deposit discipline, partially offset by “a lower contribution of free funds, driven by share repurchases and the impact of lower rates on the value of free funds.”

Outlook

  • Full-year expectations were reiterated, with Bible stating, “Our full year expectations are unchanged from the ranges we discussed in January's earnings call,” while adding, “NII is trending towards the bottom half of NII outlook of $7.2 billion to $7.35 billion, which translates into a NIM into the high 3.60s.”
  • Bible guided that “we expect both fee income and expenses to trend toward the top of their respective ranges,” and tied part of the fee outlook to mortgage sub-servicing, saying additional balances are expected “in the second half of the year.”
  • Capital positioning shifted versus the prior quarter’s framing, with Bible stating, “We are also moving to the bottom end of the CET1 ratio of 10%,” and describing the stance as supported by “continued asset quality improvement and our strong performance.”

Financial Results

  • Bible reported “net income was $664 million, compared to $759 million in the linked quarter,” alongside “M&T's net operating income was $671 million, compared to $767 million in the linked quarter.”
  • On balance sheet trends, Bible said “average loans and leases increased $0.8 billion to $138.4 billion,” with “commercial loans increased $1.5 billion to $63.8 billion” and “CRE loans declined 3% to $23.5 billion,” while “average total deposits declined $0.8 billion to $164.3 billion.”
  • Expenses rose sequentially as “noninterest expense for the quarter were $1.44 billion,” which Bible said reflected “approximately $115 million in seasonal compensation,” and he noted “the efficiency ratio was 58.3%, compared to 55.1% in the linked quarter.”
  • Credit metrics improved from the prior quarter as “net charge-offs of 31 basis points” and “criticized loans was $6.6 billion, compared to $7.3 billion at the end of December,” and Bible added, “the allowance for loan losses as a percent of total loans was unchanged at 1.53%.”

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked whether M&T would adopt ERBA and what drives the benefit; Senior EVP & CFO Bible replied, “I can't really commit to you that we will adopt the ERB,” but added, “if there's an advantage that we see here today… we would opt in, probably.”
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: pressed on why margin expectations are “a little bit below your prior expectations”; Senior EVP & CFO Bible said it reflected slower indirect consumer lending “more of a weather event,” plus timing in CRE growth, and added, “we don't want to overcommit.”
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: asked about elevated short-term borrowings; Senior EVP & CFO Bible said it reflects managing liquidity amid “a lot of volatility in deposits within our ICS business.”
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: asked why NDFI grew; Senior EVP & CFO Bible pointed to “three primary businesses,” including mortgage warehouse lending, “lending to REITs,” and “fund banking and capital call lines.”
  • Nathan Stein, Deutsche Bank: asked whether CRE balances can grow in 2Q; Senior EVP & CFO Bible responded, “I'm not going to really commit to that,” while also stating, “I know it's going to grow this year.”
  • Kenneth Usdin, Autonomous: asked about fee growth drivers and sub-servicing magnitude; Senior EVP & CFO Bible said sub-servicing could add “an annual run rate in the $30 million to $40 million range,” and added, “we may actually exceed our range.”
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: asked what is driving underwriting selectivity and where; Senior EVP & CFO Bible said competition is broad and “I'd probably lean a little bit more to structure than the pricing.”
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: asked about post-general-ledger tech spending; Senior EVP & CFO Bible said, “We actually went live on our general ledger this past weekend,” and that tech spend “just gets reallocated to another priority project.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on capital rules adoption, margin guide conservatism, and near-term CRE growth timing, with questions seeking commitments (e.g., “is it fair to say that CRE loan balances can grow in 2Q”).
  • Slightly positive management tone with repeated confidence qualifiers on performance and discipline, while maintaining caution in commitments, including “we don't want to overcommit” and “I'm not going to really commit to that.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call carried more explicit caution on near-term visibility while still reiterating full-year ranges unchanged.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Capital return accelerated, with Q1 including “$1.25 billion in share repurchases,” versus Q4’s “$507 million in share repurchases,” and management shifted the operating CET1 range to include “10%” as the lower bound, down from “10.25% to 10.5%” previously.
  • Guidance language held steady on full-year ranges, but Bible added a placement update that NII is “trending towards the bottom half” of the range, whereas Q4 framed NIM as “in the low 3.70s” for 2026.
  • Credit performance discussion improved sequentially, including lower charge-offs and reduced criticized loans in Q1, while Q4 described higher charge-offs tied to “the resolution of 3 previously identified credits.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Bible cited macro uncertainty, saying “ongoing concerns and uncertainty regarding tariffs and other policies,” and added, “The situation in Iran poses new risks to the U.S. and global economies through energy prices and uncertainty.”
  • Business risks discussed included competitive loan terms, where Bible said “structure is not something you really want to give on,” and funding dynamics, noting that “with higher rates, it's harder to get growth in our DDA accounts.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a disciplined start to 2026, anchored by modest NIM expansion, strong C&I growth, improved criticized loans and charge-offs, and a stepped-up pace of share repurchases. While reiterating full-year ranges, management emphasized that net interest income is tracking toward the bottom half of the $7.2 billion to $7.35 billion outlook and lowered the CET1 operating range to a 10% floor, alongside continued caution on near-term CRE timing and broader geopolitical and policy uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Abbott seen posting modest Q1 earnings amid Nutrition segment slowdown
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) is expected to report a modest growth in its top and bottom lines during its first quarter on April 16, before the opening bell.

EPS is expected to jump 5.5% to $1.15, while revenue is projected to rise 6.2% to $11B. Analysts do not appear very optimistic ahead of the earnings, with EPS estimates seeing 18 downward movements and no upward revisions. Revenue estimates, on the other hand, have seen 17 downward moves with just 1 upward revision.

The Nutrition business remains key, with CEO Robert Ford forecasting continued challenges in the first half of the year. The segment saw sales decline about 9% Y/Y in Q4 due to "market share loss, partly due to the loss of a large weight contract (the previous year).”

The segment is expected to return to growth in the second half, the CEO had added, benefiting from the launch of 8 new products over the course of 12 months.

For the full year, the management guided adjusted earnings per share in the range of $5.55 to $5.80, reflecting 10% growth at the midpoint, while organic sales growth is expected in the range of 6.5% to 7.5%.

Analysts will also be looking out for the management’s comments on Abbott’s acquisition of Exact Sciences (EXAS). The deal, which was initially expected to close in Q2-26, closed in March.

“EXAS, which will open a window for ABT to compete in the high-growth US cancer diagnostics market (reportedly valued at $60B), should also provide a useful fillip to ABT’s own diagnostics business, which has failed to get going for a while now with negative organic growth seen across the last 4 quarters,” Seeking Alpha analyst The Alpha Sieve noted.

Over the last 2 years, ABT has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 50% of the time.

data.title: American Tower raised to Outperform at Mizuho on undervalued data center business
data.description:
Mizuho upgraded American Tower (AMT) to Outperform from Neutral on Wednesday, based on the expectation that cell tower fundamentals are poised to inflect higher combined with the markets' underappreciation of its data center business.

Analyst Vikram Malhotra noted that the stock has underperformed in the last 12 months, falling 19% vs. REITs up 10%. 

"With the stock at 16.5x AFFO (20+% below LT avg), we believe several negatives are priced in," he wrote in a note to clients. He sees two drivers to re-rate AMT's multiples — a turnaround in cell tower fundamentals and several paths to unlocking the value of its data centers.

Mizuho points to headwinds priced into the stock, including slower U.S. organic tenant billings growth, Latin America carrier consolidation, and tech disruptions. Malhotra expects a slight uptick in activity in FY2025 driving his base case. Among potential catalysts, next year's spectrum sale can drive more network investment, he said.

The firm's sum-of-the-parts analysis indicates that  American Tower's (AMT) data center business segment is trading at ~14x EV/EBITDA, compared with pure-play data center REITs' 24x-26x.

"Avenues for unlocking value could be a) more detail on bottom-line contribution, b) additional equity partners, or c) broader strategic opportunities," Malhotra wrote.

Mizuho's Outperform rating on American Tower (AMT) disagrees with the SA Quant rating of Sell and aligns with the average SA Analyst rating and average Wall Street rating of Buy.

American Tower (AMT) stock rose 0.7% in late morning trading on Wednesday.
